items.text: Meet the controlled-not (abbreviated C-NOT) gate! As you can see, it takes two qubits. The top qubit is the control qubit, the bottom one is the target qubit. Its action is very simple. If the control qubit is blue the C-NOT doesn't change the target qubit; if the control is red the C-NOT changes the target from blue to red or from red to blue. In either case the control qubit is unchanged.
When the control qubit is blue it doesn't change the target qubit.
But when the control is red the target qubit changes from blue to red.

As long as the control and target qubits are solid colors, either red or blue, the action of the C-NOT gate is straightforward: a blue control means "do nothing to the target" and a red one means "flip the target between red and blue." And whatever happens, the control qubit is unchanged.
What do think would happen if the target qubit were purple – half blue and half red. Think about that before running the experiment.

Did you notice anything strange about that last experiment? No? Well I'll leave it to you to experiment but here are some questions to get you started.

What happens when the control qubit is purple and the target qubit is red?
What happens when they're both purple?
What if they're some other color –  closer to red or closer to blue?

Do you think you could reproduce these results using just the flipper gates?
Now there are two qubits, two flipper gates and a wide magnifying glass that measures both qubits at once. This is how it works.

Try running this with different settings on the two flipper gates. Do you see how that affects the two-qubit count? Try running qubits through those gates multiple times before making the measurement. Once you understand this behavior go to the first Entanglement tab and things will start getting strange.

Play around with the flipper gate settings until you are sure that you understand what they're doing to the two qubits. IMPORTANT: Just as a qubit is an object that can be in two states at once, try to think of the two qubits as though they are a single object with four states – blue-blue, blue-red, red-blue, and red-red – with different probabilities. For the moment, the qubits are independewnt of each other and the probabilities of the pair of qubits are just the product of the probabilities for each qubit, but that will change when the qubits are entangled.

People often talk about qubits as though they are like bits except that somehow "they can be a zero and one all at once," whatever that means. And then they try to explain what it means by talking about flipping coins or some such. That analogy is incomplete and, worse, it's misleading. There are some similarities, though, so let's start there.


Coins have two states – heads and tails. Qubits can also be in one of two sates. These are commonly known as "zero" and "one" but in Qubit Lab we use two colors: blue and red.

So that blue circle you see on the left there? That's a qubit in the blue state. And the funny looking tank-like object next to it? We call that a flipper because, in analogy to flipping a coin, it acts on a qubit to change its state. Click on Show me to see how it works.

As you saw, the qubit changed to a purplish color – something in between blue and red. (For now, ignore the + sign.)  What does that mean? You see that magnifying glass? That's not an ordinary magnifying glass, it's actually a tool for measuring the state of a qubit. Click Show me to see how it works.

Whenever you measure a qubit it will always be either blue or red, never in between, just as a coin always lands on heads or tails. So if you really want to know the qubit's state you have to run that measurement more than once. Qubit Lab makes that easy to do. Once you've measured a qubit, whatever you did to it is saved and you can run the experiment again by clicking on the magnifying glass. If you want to run the experiment multiple times just change the number at the bottom of the magnifying glass. Click "Show me" and I'll do both those things.
If you want to run the experiment hundreds or thousands of times, don't worry – we won't run the animation and it won't take very long!
Before we go, I want to show something really strange about qubits. You can change their state as often as you like before you measure them. You can even "unflip" them that way! Watch this!

Did you see what happened there?  You couldn't do that with a coin!

OK, it's tme for you to explore Qubit Lab on your own. You can drag the qubit to the flipper or the magnifying glass and you can change the flipper to a different setting to see what that does to the qubit. For the time being you can ignore the + and - signs. They make no difference for a single qubit.

You've seen that that innocent looking C-NOT gate can do something intereeting two the qubits when the control qubit is neither red nor blue but is in some mixed state. In that situation, even if the target qubit starts out, say, blue it ends up in a mixed state but when the two qubits are measured they're always either both blue (because a blue control leaves the blue target alone) or both red (because a red control changes the target to red). So you never get a blue-red or a red-blue measurement.


In this situation the two qubits are said to be entangled.

Up to now we have been encouraging you to think of a pair of qubits as a single object – that's why we've been using a double-qubit magnifying glass to measure them both simultaneously. On this tab we're going back to a single magnifier but nothing else has changed. Why did we even bother to make this change? I thought you'd never ask!...

Remember when you were experimenting with just one qubit how you could change its state as often as you liked before you made the measurement? You could even "unflip" the qubit and return it to its original state! What if you did the same thing with a pair of entangled qubits? What if you changed the state of one of the qubits before making the measurement? What would happen?

That went by pretty quickly! Do you understand what happened? 

Just as a single qubit can be in a mixture of two states, blue and red, an entangled pair can be in a mixture of four states: blue-blue, blue-red, red-blue, and red-red. In the experiment you just saw, the C-NOT gate put the pair into an equal mixture of blue-blue and red-red, but then the flipper gate changed one of the qubits to red and that forced the pair into the red-red condition (That's the equivalent, by the way, of what you did way back when you "unflipped" a single qubit and changed it from a purple state into red or blue and the measurement ceased to be random.)

Experiment on your own now and try to predict the outcome of each experiment before you run it. And while you're doing that think about this question:

What would happen if you sent one of your pair of qubits off to someone very far away, say on Mars? If she measured her qubit what would happen to yours? We can't do that yet in Qubit Lab but it's on the wish list (well, maybe not the Mars part!).
